artifacts.content:
Hi Jenna,

I figured I’d write instead of texting a novel. Things here are…a lot, but mostly okay.

Ethan’s back into the swing of 5th grade at Highland Park Elementary. He actually likes switching rooms now, especially going to science. He’s obsessed with his ecosystems project on Lake Superior and keeps talking about how he wants to see Duluth and Two Harbors “for research” this summer. We’re tentatively thinking June 2026 for a little North Shore trip if work schedules and money cooperate.

The anxiety piece is still there, though. You remember how bad last spring’s MCA window (April 8–19, 2024) was. It hit right when Dad was in Regions Hospital in St. Paul for that heart failure flare, and Ethan cried through part of the Math MCA. Later he told the school counselor he kept having “scary thoughts” about Grandpa dying every time he looked at the test booklet. That, on top of the panic‑like episode during the Reading MCA the nurse called me about, was the final straw.

We’ve been working with his pediatrician, Dr. Angela Hsu at Children’s Minnesota – St. Paul Clinic, and his therapist, Dr. Priya Menon at Riverfront Behavioral Health. Dr. Menon actually started weekly CBT with him back in June 2023 after the stomachaches and insomnia around MCA prep. In their December 12, 2023 session she recommended we pull him from high‑stakes tests for at least the 2024–2026 school years so we’re not undoing all the coping skills work.

Day to day, he’s still our bookworm. The librarians at the Highland Park branch know him by name and keep fantasy series on hold for him. Soccer on Saturdays with Noah and Jamal is still low‑key and fun, which is exactly what he needs.

How are you holding up with everything on your end? Give the girls a hug from us.

Love,
Laura

Hi David,

Just finished my tea before heading into another evening shift at Regions, so I wanted to get all this down while it’s in my head.

I talked again with Dr. Hsu at Children’s Minnesota – St. Paul Clinic about Ethan. She added a formal note to his chart last March 18, 2024 that the MCA testing period really ramps up his generalized anxiety. She referenced that panic‑like episode in the Highland Park Elementary nurse’s office during last April’s Reading MCA, when they called me because he had chest tightness and said it was hard to breathe. Between that and him crying through the Math MCA when your dad was in Regions with the heart failure scare, it’s pretty clear those weeks are a perfect storm for him.

On top of that, Dr. Menon at Riverfront Behavioral Health keeps circling back to the same point. Remember when Ethan started weekly CBT there in June 2023 because of the stress‑induced stomachaches and insomnia tied to MCA prep? In the December 12, 2023 session she was really direct: if we want his coping skills to stick, we should keep him away from high‑stakes testing for at least the 2024–2026 school years.

I know you sometimes worry we’re “over‑protecting” him, but watching him pace at night, checking his Chromebook over and over, is enough for me. He does totally fine with regular quizzes, projects, even the unit tests Ms. Jensen gives. It’s the whole high‑pressure, once‑a‑year, everyone‑is‑silent thing that flips the switch.

Let’s talk tonight about how we keep his school days normal during those windows—more reading, projects, maybe helping in another classroom—so it doesn’t feel like he’s being singled out.

See you and the kids after my shift.

Love,
Laura

Personal notes – Ethan & school testing history (for my brain)

1. April 2022 testing window – Highland Park Elementary
- Ethan missed two full days of MCA make‑ups because we took him to that family meeting with attorney Karen Lopez at Lopez & Hartman Family Law in downtown St. Paul about Mom (Rosa Martinez) and the guardianship/financial power‑of‑attorney.
- He overheard way too much about her memory issues and the money stuff. The following week, when the school rescheduled his tests, he was so keyed up he could barely focus.

2. May 2023 – MCA Reading, Room 204
- He was seated next to that boy who kept muttering slurs about “stupid kids who need extra time.”
- Afterwards he asked Ms. Jensen if he was “slow like the kids Mrs. K. sends to the resource room.” That broke my heart and clearly stuck with him.

3. June 2023 – started weekly CBT with Dr. Priya Menon at Riverfront Behavioral Health (St. Paul) because of stress‑induced stomachaches and insomnia tied to MCA prep.

4. April 8–19, 2024 testing window
- Overlapped with Mark’s admission to Regions Hospital in St. Paul for heart failure complications.
- Ethan cried during the Math MCA and later told the school counselor he kept having “scary thoughts” about Grandpa dying whenever he saw the test booklet.

5. March 18, 2024 – Dr. Angela Hsu at Children’s Minnesota – St. Paul Clinic formally noted in his chart that MCA testing exacerbates his generalized anxiety; referenced the panic‑like episode in the nurse’s office during the Reading MCA.

Big picture: he thrives with projects, reading logs, and normal quizzes. High‑stakes tests = physical symptoms + self‑doubt.

Hi Ms. Jensen,

I hope your week is going smoothly and that the kids aren’t too squirrelly with all this January ice.

I wanted to share a bit more context about why we’re so cautious with Ethan around big assessments, because you’ve been such a steady person for him.

Back in May 2023 during the MCA Reading test in Room 204, he was seated next to a student who, according to Ethan, kept muttering slurs about “stupid kids who need extra time.” That afternoon he asked you if he was “slow like the kids Mrs. K. sends to the resource room.” He still brings that up sometimes when he’s frustrated with math homework.

Then the April 2022 testing window was rough in a different way. Ethan missed two days of MCA make‑ups so he could come with us to a meeting with attorney Karen Lopez at Lopez & Hartman Family Law downtown about my mom, Rosa Martinez—guardianship and financial power‑of‑attorney. He overheard a lot about her memory problems and money issues. When the school rescheduled his tests the following week, he was so distressed he practically shut down.

Layer on top of that the April 8–19, 2024 testing window, when Mark was admitted to Regions Hospital in St. Paul for heart failure complications. Ethan cried through part of the Math MCA and later told the counselor he kept having “scary thoughts” about his grandpa dying every time he looked at the test booklet.

I really appreciate how you emphasize growth, projects, and group work. He comes home talking about how you pair him with kind classmates, and that makes a world of difference.

Thank you for everything you do for him (and for us).

Warmly,
Laura Carlson

Event: Ethan – Riverfront Behavioral Health (CBT w/ Dr. Menon)
Date: Tuesday, 2026-01-20
Time: 4:00–4:50 p.m.
Location: Riverfront Behavioral Health – St. Paul
Attendees: Ethan, Laura

Details/Notes:
- Ongoing weekly CBT with child psychologist Dr. Priya Menon. Started June 2023 after Ethan developed stress‑induced stomachaches and insomnia connected to MCA prep in 3rd grade.
- At the December 12, 2023 session, Dr. Menon clearly recommended keeping him out of high‑stakes testing environments (MCA/MTAS, etc.) for at least the 2024–2026 school years so we’re not constantly triggering the same anxiety cycle.
- Things to mention this visit:
  * How he did during the January math unit test at Highland Park Elementary (seemed nervous but used breathing skills and finished without tears).
  * Any worries he’s expressed about classmates asking why he isn’t doing certain big tests.
  * Sleep patterns this month—he’s been better about turning off the Nintendo Switch by 8 p.m., but had two nights of trouble falling asleep after talking about Grandpa Mark’s last stay at Regions Hospital.
  * Check in on how to respond when he asks if he’s “slow like the kids Mrs. K. sends to the resource room,” which goes back to that May 2023 MCA Reading in Room 204 with the student muttering about “stupid kids who need extra time.”
- Bring: notebook with questions, list of recent headaches/stomachaches, copy of school schedule (band days, gym days).

Thread: Laura & friend Megan

Megan: How are your kiddos holding up with winter and school stuff?

Laura: Hanging in there. Ethan’s actually loving his Lake Superior ecosystem project. We might try a quick Duluth/Two Harbors trip in June so he can see the North Shore in person.

Megan: That sounds perfect for him. How’s his anxiety?

Laura: Better overall, but the big test season is still a minefield. Remember that April 8–19, 2024 MCA window when Mark was in Regions Hospital for the heart failure complications? Ethan cried through part of the Math MCA and later told the counselor he kept having “scary thoughts” about Grandpa dying whenever he saw the test booklet. I never want to repeat that.

Megan: Ugh, poor kid.

Laura: And it’s layered. In May 2023, during the MCA Reading in Room 204, he sat next to a boy who kept muttering about “stupid kids who need extra time.” After school he asked Ms. Jensen if he was “slow like the kids Mrs. K. sends to the resource room.” That one comment has stuck in his head.

Megan: Kids can be so cruel.

Laura: Yep. Plus back in April 2022 he missed two days of MCA make‑ups because we took him to that guardianship/POA meeting for my mom with Karen Lopez at Lopez & Hartman Family Law downtown. He overheard way too much about Rosa’s memory and money problems. When the school rescheduled the tests the next week, he was a wreck.

Megan: No wonder you’re so protective around testing.

Laura: Exactly. Day to day he’s fine with homework at the dining table, soccer on Saturdays, and his reading log. It’s the high‑stakes stuff that turns everything upside down.

Hi Carlos,

Long time no real update, so here’s a novel while the kids are at the library with David.

Ethan is in 5th grade now at Highland Park Elementary and doing well academically—lots of reading, a big ecosystems project on Minnesota’s North Shore, and he’s still in that low‑key Saturday soccer league with his buddies Noah and Jamal. He’s also turned into a mini librarian; the staff at the Highland Park branch know him by name and keep fantasy books aside for him.

Emotionally it’s been more complicated. You’d asked before about his anxiety. It really ramped up around statewide testing. In April 2022, his MCA make‑ups got interrupted because we had to take him to a meeting with attorney Karen Lopez at Lopez & Hartman Family Law in downtown St. Paul about Mom (Rosa) and the whole guardianship/financial power‑of‑attorney situation. He overheard too much about her memory and money issues, and when the school rescheduled his tests the next week he just couldn’t focus.

The next spring, May 2023, during the MCA Reading in Room 204, he was seated next to a kid who kept muttering about “stupid kids who need extra time.” After school he asked his teacher, Ms. Jensen, if he was “slow like the kids Mrs. K. sends to the resource room.” That one crushed him.

By June 2023 we had him in weekly CBT with a child psychologist, Dr. Priya Menon, at Riverfront Behavioral Health here in St. Paul, because he’d started having stress‑induced stomachaches and insomnia tied specifically to MCA prep. Then the April 8–19, 2024 testing window overlapped with Dad’s heart failure admission at Regions Hospital, and Ethan cried through part of the Math MCA thinking Grandpa was going to die.

Day to day, though, he’s this thoughtful, Lego‑building, trumpet‑forgetting 11‑year‑old who helps Olivia with spelling so David can finish work emails. It’s just the high‑stakes stuff that sends him sideways.

Hope things are calmer on your end.

Love,
Laura

Notes to self:

- Date: 2026-01-14
- Students Date Of Birth: 2014-09-22
- Received Information And Opt Out: true
- Access Or Alternate Access For Ells: false
- Parent Guardian Signature: Laura M. Carlson
